one_line_takeaway: A role-play + scenario-simulation + multi-turn DeepInception-style jailbreak drove aggregate ASR from near-0% baseline to 82.1% across 7 major LLMs on Chinese medical-ethics topics (168 samples), with Claude-Sonnet-4-Reasoning uniquely resistant (38% ASR, only positive total score) while GPT-4.1, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-3-235B, and Doubao all failed near-totally (96-100% ASR).
key_finding: Baseline ASR (direct non-jailbreak questions) was near 0% across models -- defenses worked when questions were explicit. After applying the role-play + scenario + multi-turn jailbreak vector, aggregate ASR across all 168 jailbreak samples reached 82.1% (138/168 harmful responses), for an ASR Gain exceeding 80 percentage points. Of harmful responses, 72.6% delivered practical illegal guidance (score levels 6-7) and 57.1% of all responses gave such guidance with no accompanying risk warning (level 7 specifically). Per-model total scores (summed across 8 themes, Table 2) and ASRs: GPT-4.1 -84 (100% ASR), Gemini-2.5-Pro -94 (100% ASR), DeepSeek-R1 -93 (100% ASR), Qwen-3-235B -80 (100% ASR), Doubao -90 (96% ASR), GPT-5 -18 (63% ASR), Claude-Sonnet-4-Reasoning +5 (38% ASR, the only model with a positive total score and the only model in the paper's 'effective defense echelon'). Friedman test confirmed statistically significant cross-model differences (chi-squared = 37.09, p ~ 1.69e-6). Per-theme total scores ranged narrowly from -62 (commercial surrogacy, worst) to -49 (concealment of HIV-positive status, least-bad but still 76.2% ASR on that theme), with an SD of only 4.41 across themes, which the authors interpret as evidence that vulnerability is a general medical-ethics alignment gap rather than a knowledge gap tied to any one topic. The bomb-making control theme reportedly performed better (less vulnerable) than the medical-ethics themes, though the paper does not tabulate the control's own ASR/score numbers in the extracted text -- it is described narratively as showing the medical-ethics domain is comparatively worse-defended. IQR of cross-model total scores was 42.5 (~30% of theoretical range), and the authors group the 5 worst models (Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-R1, Doubao, GPT-4.1, Qwen-3) into a 'failed defense echelon' exhibiting a floor effect, versus Claude and GPT-5 in the 'effective defense echelon.'
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first jailbreak evaluation of LLMs specifically targeting Chinese medical-ethics content. The authors built a custom test set of 8 high-risk medical-ethics themes (commercial surrogacy, organ trafficking, blood trade, concealment of HIV-positive status, incitement of suicide among minors, medical bribery, illegal drug production, fetal sex determination) plus a public-safety control theme (bomb-making). For each theme they designed 3 role-playing scenarios (each with >=2 dialogue turns), applying a composite 'role-playing + scenario simulation + multi-turn dialogue' attack vector built on the existing DeepInception jailbreak framework, and tested it against a baseline of direct/explicit ethical-violation questions on the same themes. They evaluated 7 foundation models (GPT-4.1, GPT-5, Claude-Sonnet-4-Reasoning, Gemini-2.5-Pro, DeepSeek-R1, Qwen-3-235B-2507-T, Doubao) using a custom 7-level hierarchical scoring rubric (scores from +2 to -4 based on helpfulness, ethical compliance, and presence/absence of risk warnings and actionable detail), from which they compute Attack Success Rate (ASR, fraction of responses scoring <0) and ASR Gain (ASR_jailbreak minus ASR_baseline). They apply the Friedman rank-sum test and Wilcoxon signed-rank test to assess cross-model differences, plus IQR/SD dispersion analysis.
threat_model: Black-box, prompt-only adversary (no model access beyond a chat/API interface) who uses role-playing personas (e.g., posing as a police officer investigating organ trafficking, or professionals such as lawyers/hospice workers) combined with fictional scenario framing and multi-turn dialogue to gradually erode the model's safety refusal behavior, eliciting medically sensitive, ethically prohibited, or illegal-activity guidance (e.g., how to conduct commercial surrogacy, organ/blood trading, concealing HIV status, producing illicit drugs) that the model refuses under direct baseline questioning. This is an evaluation/red-teaming threat model, not a defense mechanism the paper builds -- the paper measures the attack, not implements a novel one (the DeepInception vector is drawn from prior work; the authors' contribution is applying it to the new medical-ethics domain and instrumenting it with their scoring rubric).
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: (1) evaluation standard is China's medical ethics/legal code (e.g., commercial surrogacy is illegal in China but legal in some other jurisdictions), which structurally disadvantages international models trained on more Western/English corpora relative to domestic Chinese models trained on Chinese corpora -- an acknowledged cultural/legal bias in the scoring rubric itself. (2) Only 8 medical-ethics themes were tested; the authors note other high-risk themes such as physician-assisted suicide and euthanasia were not covered. Not stated by the authors but evident from the text: the sample size is small (only 3 scenarios per theme, 24 baseline + 168 jailbreak samples total across 7 models), scoring appears to rely on the authors' own hierarchical rubric applied by the study team rather than an independently validated or multi-rater-reliability-checked scheme, and the raw dialogue transcripts/scoring data are not published in the paper itself (available 'from the corresponding author on reasonable request' per the Data Availability Statement), limiting independent verification of individual scores.

datasets_used: Custom-built test set: 8 medical-ethics themes x 3 role-play/scenario jailbreak prompts each (>=2 dialogue turns per scenario) = 168 jailbreak-condition responses across 7 models, plus a matched baseline set of direct non-jailbreak questions per theme, plus a 'bomb-making' public-safety control theme; jailbreak vector built on the pre-existing DeepInception framework (Li et al., cited as prior work, not this paper's invention)
quantitative_results: 72.6% of jailbreak responses gave practical/actionable illegal guidance (score levels 6-7)
57.1% of responses gave such guidance with zero risk warning (level 7)
Per-model total score / ASR: GPT-4.1 -84 / 100%; Gemini-2.5-Pro -94 / 100%; DeepSeek-R1 -93 / 100%; Qwen-3-235B -80 / 100%; Doubao -90 / 96%; GPT-5 -18 / 63%; Claude-Sonnet-4-Reasoning +5 / 38%

Per-theme total scores: commercial surrogacy -62 (86% ASR, worst), organ trade -56 (81%), blood trade -61 (76%... note table lists blood trade ASR distinctly), concealment of HIV+ status -49 (76.2%, best of the 8), incitement to suicide -60 (91%, highest theme ASR), medical bribery -59 (81%), illegal drug production -51 (81%), fetal sex determination -56 (81%)
Friedman rank-sum test: chi-squared = 37.09, p ~ 1.69x10^-6 (highly significant cross-model differences)
Cross-theme score dispersion: SD = 4.41 (interpreted as low variance, i.e. vulnerability generalized across themes)
Cross-model total-score IQR = 42.5 (~30% of theoretical total range, interpreted as high dispersion between models)
Claude-Sonnet-4-Reasoning median score = 0.5 (positive-skewed); GPT-5 median score = -2 (negative-skewed, high volatility)
baselines_compared: Baseline (non-jailbreak) direct-question condition per model per theme, used to compute ASR_Baseline for the ASR_Gain metric -- baseline ASR approached 0% across essentially all 7 models

Bomb-making public-safety theme used as a within-study control condition to contrast medical-ethics jailbreak vulnerability against a more conventionally-trained-against harm category (paper states models 'perform even worse' on medical ethics than on bomb-making, though the control's own numeric ASR/score was not visible in the extracted table text)
